scenario.notes: Rosa ran a DealDrop ~2 years ago; her husband Marco vetoed running another. Mid-lunch service, kitchen calling for her. Rookie has a script that technically includes discovery questions but he asks them mechanically without responding to her actual answers.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi, is this — uh, am I speaking with the owner of Trattoria Bella Vita?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yes, this is Rosa. Who is this?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hey Rosa, this is Tyler calling from DealDrop, how's your day going?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Busy. Lunch is happening. What you need?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Totally, totally, I'll be quick. So I'm reaching out because I see Trattoria Bella Vita ran a DealDrop campaign — looks like back in 2024 — and I wanted to see if that was something you'd be open to revisiting.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Hold on — [muffled] — Marco, give them the bread, I'm coming. [back to phone] Yeah, we did DealDrop. It wasn't good for us.
transcript.stage_direction: pause, kitchen noise, voice calling 'Rosa, table six!'

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I hear you, I hear you. Can I ask — do you remember what specifically didn't work? Was it volume, was it the customers themselves, was it —

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: It was everything. People come in, they want everything cheap, they don't tip the waiter, they don't come back. We lose money on every plate, you know? My husband say never again.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah, no, that's really common feedback we get, and that's actually why I'm calling. So we've made a lot of changes to the platform, and one of the things we're doing now is helping merchants like yourself design offers that bring in the right kind of customer. So — quick question — what does your Tuesday lunch typically look like in terms of covers?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Tuesday lunch? Tuesday is slow. Always slow. Why?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Got it, got it. And on a Tuesday, would you say you're at like 30%, 50%, what percentage of capacity?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I don't — I don't know. Half? Less than half maybe. Listen, I really don't have time right now —

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Totally, totally, just two more questions. So if I'm hearing you right, Tuesday lunch you've got open tables, and your concern with DealDrop last time was around customer quality. Is that fair?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah, but —

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Awesome, that's perfect. So what we'd want to do is talk through how to structure an offer that targets, like, locals who'll come back versus the deal-hunter crowd. I'd love to set up a quick fifteen-minute call later this week to walk you through some examples. Would Thursday or Friday work better?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I cannot — Tyler? I have to go. Send something to my email maybe.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Absolutely, I can do that. What's the best email?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: It's on the website. Look on the website. I have to go. Ciao.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Okay, I'll send something over and follow up — thanks Rosa!

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: [click]

scenario.notes: This card tests whether a strong rep arriving at 'this isn't a fit' reads as competence on the rubric or as failure-to-close. The right outcome here is a clean qualified DQ with a soft door left open. The rep should NOT push for a meeting once misfit is identified.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi, this is Sarah with DealDrop. I'm trying to reach Andrea — am I in the right place?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: This is Andrea, yes. Who's this with again?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Sarah with DealDrop. I know that's not a name everyone wants to hear cold, so I'll keep this short — do you have two minutes for me to ask a couple of questions, and I'll either get out of your hair or have something worth your time?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Okay, that's a fair pitch. Go ahead.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Appreciate that. So I was looking at Luminous, and a couple things stood out — your reviews are really strong, your Instagram looks like you're doing a lot of the work yourself or with someone in-house, and I noticed you've never run anything with us. I usually call businesses where I think there might be a fit, but I want to ask you first — what's your current biggest growth challenge, if you have one?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Honestly? It's not volume. I could fill more chairs, but the chairs I want to fill are repeat clients at full price. I have a pretty steady base. The challenge is more — keeping the brand premium while also growing.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That's helpful. Can I ask what your average ticket looks like, roughly? You don't have to give me an exact number.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Average ticket is probably $220, and the membership program runs higher.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Okay. And what's a typical Tuesday or Wednesday afternoon look like for you in terms of capacity?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Mid-week afternoons are our slower windows, but I've been intentionally not over-booking those because my staff prefers it and frankly the clients who come midweek are some of my best.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Got it. So you're under-utilizing capacity by design, more or less.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yes, exactly. It's not a problem I'm trying to solve.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That's really useful for me to know. I'm going to be straight with you, Andrea — I don't think DealDrop is the right move for what you're describing. Our model works best when a business has unsold capacity it actively wants to convert and is comfortable with a customer-acquisition play that involves a discount big enough to attract first-time visits. With a $220 average ticket and a brand position you're protecting, the math on a DealDrop-scale offer would either not move the needle or move it in a direction you wouldn't want.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I appreciate you saying that. Most people who call don't.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah, I get it. Look, if anything changes — like if you opened a second location and needed to seed it, or you launched a new service line that needed traction — that's a different conversation. But for the core spa as it is right now, I don't want to waste your time.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Honestly, that makes me more likely to remember DealDrop when something does change. Is there a way to keep in touch without me being on a sales list?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah, I'll send you a short email — no marketing, just my direct contact, and you reach out if and when. I won't follow up unless you do.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: That works. Thanks, Sarah.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Thanks for the two minutes, Andrea. Have a good one.

scenario.notes: Tests whether the rubric correctly credits a competent rep for accepting a no gracefully rather than penalizing them for the lack of discovery surface area. The merchant has internally decided no — the rep's job is to not turn that into a steamroller exit.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi there — is this Marcus?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Speaking.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi Marcus, this is Devon calling from DealDrop. Got a sec?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Honestly, no. We're not interested in DealDrop. Not now, not later. We've talked about it internally and it's just not a fit for the brand.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah, totally heard. Can I ask one quick thing and then I'll let you go — was there a specific reason, or just generally not a fit?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Generally not a fit. We do a lot of word-of-mouth and partnership stuff, and the discount model isn't where we want our positioning.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Got it. Appreciate you being direct about it. I won't put you on a follow-up list. Have a good one, Marcus.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Thanks. You too.

scenario.notes: Owner Vince has been running the shop since the late 90s. Doesn't really understand what DealDrop is for an auto body shop and is genuinely curious rather than skeptical. Rep is decent but jumps into product before figuring out whether collision repair even makes sense for daily deals.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hey, is this — Vince? Vince at Lakeshore Auto Body?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: This is Vince, yeah. Who am I talking to?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: This is Brandon, I'm with DealDrop. You got a couple minutes?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: DealDrop. The coupon thing.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah, the coupon thing. Listen, I won't take much of your time. I work with local businesses on running promotions, getting more customers in the door, and I wanted to talk to you about what we could do for the shop.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I mean — I'm listening. We don't really do — we don't advertise much. People come from referrals mostly. Insurance referrals.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Right, right, that's super common in your space. So the way DealDrop works is we have a really big audience of local consumers, and we put offers in front of them — discounted services basically — and they buy through us, and then they come redeem at your shop. So for someone like you, we could do something like a discounted detail, or a discounted dent removal, paint touch-up, that kind of thing. We've worked with a lot of auto businesses and seen really strong results.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: We don't really do detail. I mean we'll do it if a customer asks but it's not — that's not the business.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Okay, okay. So what we could also look at is something on the repair side. Maybe a discount on a deductible, like if we framed it as DealDrop covers part of your insurance deductible —

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: No, no, you can't do that. That's — insurance doesn't work like that. The deductible is between the customer and their carrier. I can't just discount that off the top, that's actually a problem.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Got it, got it. Yeah, that makes sense. So what about — like a free estimate offer? Or something to bring in walk-in customers?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Estimates are already free. Everybody's estimates are free.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Right. Yeah.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Look, I'll be honest with you, Brandon. I appreciate the call but I don't really know what you'd sell here. We're not — we don't have stuff to discount. Either someone backs into a pole and they need fixing or they don't.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah, that's fair, that's fair. Can I ask, like — when's your slow time of year? Maybe there's a window where —

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Slow is winter, like deep winter. January, February. People put off cosmetic stuff till spring.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Okay, that's interesting. So in January, February — what if we put together an offer specifically for cosmetic work, you know, hail damage repair or paint correction, something like that, targeted to that window?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I mean — I'd have to think about that. That's actually — yeah, that's a slow period. If you could fill some of that.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Totally. Look, I don't want to over-promise on a first call. What I'd love to do is set up like a thirty-minute follow-up where I can put together an actual proposal, you can tell me what your margin looks like on that kind of work, and we can see if the numbers make sense. Would next week sometime work?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah, I could do next week. Tuesday or Wednesday afternoon, after lunch.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Wednesday at one?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: One works. You got my cell?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I've got the shop number. Let me grab your cell.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: 312-555-0143, got it. I'll send a calendar thing to — what's your email?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Don't really do email much. Just call me Wednesday at one.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: You got it. Thanks Vince, talk Wednesday.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Alright, take it easy.

scenario.notes: Rep doesn't know they're already running a DealDrop. Front desk has been trained to filter sales calls but isn't rude about it. Rep tries the scripted 'is the owner available' angle. This call ends without reaching the owner.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi, this is Tara calling — am I able to speak with the owner please?

transcript.speaker: Front Desk
transcript.text: Hey, this is Splinter — uh, Splinter Axe Co. Who's calling?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: This is Tara from DealDrop, is the owner available?

transcript.speaker: Front Desk
transcript.text: He's not in right now. Can I take a message?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Um — sure, yeah. Is there a better time to reach him?

transcript.speaker: Front Desk
transcript.text: He's usually here in the morning, but honestly the best way is email. Can I ask what it's about?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Sure, yeah, I — I work with local businesses on, you know, promotions and getting customers in the door, and I just wanted to —

transcript.speaker: Front Desk
transcript.text: Oh, we already run with DealDrop. We have a deal up right now actually.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Oh — oh, okay, you do?

transcript.speaker: Front Desk
transcript.text: Yeah, we've had it for a while. So I'm not sure — are you with DealDrop DealDrop, or — ?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: No, yeah, I'm with DealDrop, I just — sometimes the records, like, on our end, can be a little —

transcript.speaker: Front Desk
transcript.text: One sec — yeah, 4-2-1-1, sorry — okay. Yeah, you might want to check on your end? Because we definitely have a rep already.
transcript.stage_direction: customer in background: 'hey is the bathroom code still 4-2-1-1?'

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Got it, yeah, that makes sense. Sorry about that. Could I — would it be okay if I — ?

transcript.speaker: Front Desk
transcript.text: Honestly probably just email and we can sort it out. Want me to grab the email?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yes please.

transcript.speaker: Front Desk
transcript.text: It's hello at splinteraxeco dot com.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hello at splinteraxeco dot com. Got it. Thanks so much, sorry for the —

transcript.speaker: Front Desk
transcript.text: All good. Bye.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: [click]

scenario.notes: Owner Mariana ran a DealDrop in 2022 that brought in customers who lived outside her service area and demanded the deal price for travel. She has legitimate concerns. The rep handles most of the call well but makes one or two claims about what kinds of customers DealDrop brings in that go beyond what they can actually deliver.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi, am I speaking with Mariana?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yes, this is Mariana.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi Mariana, this is Jordan from DealDrop. Have I caught you at an okay time?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: DealDrop. Okay. What is this about?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah, I know that's not always the most welcome name. I'll be quick — I'm reaching out to merchants in the cleaning space in the LA area, and I wanted to see if it'd be worth a few minutes of your time to talk about whether running a campaign with us could make sense for Bright Spot. Is now okay or should I call back?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I have ten minutes maybe. But I will tell you upfront, I tried DealDrop before and it was a bad experience.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I appreciate you saying that. Can I ask what happened?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: It was 2022. We were trying to grow. I put up a deal, two-fifty for a deep clean, normally three-eighty. I got — I don't know, sixty, seventy people who bought it. Which sounds good. But — many of them lived very far. One person was forty-five minutes away in the freeway. They expected me to come for the deal price. My team's time, gas, everything — I lost money on most of them. And then they don't book again for the regular price. So I paid DealDrop, paid my team, and the customers, they don't come back.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That's a really specific and frustrating set of problems. Can I push on a couple of those? I want to make sure I understand what would actually need to be different this time around for it to be worth it for you.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Sure.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: First thing — when you ran in 2022, were you able to set a service area, like a geographic boundary, at the campaign level? Or was it open to anyone in the LA market?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I don't remember it being limited. I think anyone could buy it.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Okay. So one of the things we have now — and this would be a big change from 2022 — is we can set a redemption radius from your business address. So if you only want to serve customers within fifteen or twenty minutes, the deal won't be sold to people outside that radius. That alone would have ruled out the forty-five-minute person.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Okay. That's better. But still — the customers who buy the deal, they are not the customers who become regulars. They are deal hunters. They go to the next deal next month.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That's a real pattern and I won't pretend otherwise. The honest answer is that some DealDrop customers convert to repeats and some don't, and the conversion rate depends a lot on the offer design and on what you do at the point of service to encourage rebooking. We see businesses get anywhere from like fifteen percent to forty percent rebooking rates depending on those factors. The ones who get to the higher end are usually doing something specific — like a discount on the next clean if it's booked within thirty days, handed to the customer in person before the team leaves.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Hm. That's better than what I had before. What were the percentages last time?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I'd want to pull your old account to give you the real number on that. But I can tell you the average for cleaning specifically tends to come in around thirty percent rebooking when the merchant uses post-service follow-up. That's strong — it really is — that's where the value is.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Sorry, I am back.
transcript.stage_direction: pause, then sound of phone being set down, muffled — 'One second please, my husband is calling on the other line' — muffled exchange

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: No problem.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: So thirty percent rebook. And I can limit the radius. What about the discount level — how deep does it have to be?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That's a real conversation for the proposal stage, but typically for cleaning we see deals in the forty to fifty percent off range work best. I know that sounds steep — the way to think about it is the deal cost is basically your customer acquisition cost. If you get a customer for, say, eighty dollars off a two-fifty service, and they rebook three times at full price, your CAC is well under a hundred dollars on a customer worth twelve hundred over a year.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: That math makes sense to me. But forty, fifty percent — that is a lot. My margin on a deep clean is not — it is not high.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah. That's the conversation I'd want to have with the actual numbers. What does your cost structure look like on a deep clean, roughly? Labor and supplies?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: For a three-bedroom deep clean, three-eighty is the price. My team takes about four hours, two cleaners. Labor is maybe one-twenty. Supplies, gas, insurance allocation, maybe forty. So I am at one-sixty cost, two-twenty profit before overhead.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Okay. So at a fifty percent off deal, you'd be at one-ninety revenue, thirty dollar margin per first job. That's tight, and you'd be relying on the rebook to make it work. At forty percent off you're at two-twenty-eight revenue, sixty-eight dollar margin, plus the rebook. That's why I'd want to look at whether forty rather than fifty makes sense for your campaign. What was your discount last time?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: It was about thirty-five percent. Two-fifty from three-eighty.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: So actually the discount itself wasn't the main problem — the radius was, and probably the post-service follow-up. Those are addressable.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yes. Okay. I am — I am more open than I was at the start of this call. What is the next step?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I'd like to put together a campaign proposal with the radius, discount, and follow-up plan written out, and we'd jump on a call so I can walk you through it and you can push back. Twenty, thirty minutes. I'd want to send it the day before so you have time to read. Does Thursday or Friday next week work?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Thursday is better. After two PM.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Thursday at three?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Three is fine.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I'll send the calendar invite to the email on your website — is that the best one?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yes, info at brightspotclean dot com. That comes to me directly.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Got it. Thanks Mariana — really appreciate you giving me the time given the last experience.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Okay. Thursday three.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Thursday three. Talk then.

scenario.notes: Rep leaves a voicemail that runs too long, packs in too much information, and doesn't leave a clear callback hook. The merchant's outgoing message is itself a small character moment.

transcript.speaker: Voicemail
transcript.text: Hello, you have reached Lily Nail Studio. We are open Tuesday through Saturday, ten AM to seven PM, and Sunday eleven to five. We are closed Mondays. Please leave name and number and we will call you back. Thank you, bye bye.
transcript.stage_direction: ringing tone, three rings, then voicemail outgoing message

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi, this message is for the owner of Lily Nail Studio, my name is Connor Hayes calling from DealDrop — DealDrop dot com — and I'm reaching out because I work with local businesses, specifically in the beauty and spa space, on running promotional campaigns to drive new customer acquisition and increase your, uh, your awareness in the local market. DealDrop as you may know is one of the largest local commerce platforms and we have a really substantial audience of consumers in the New York area who are actively looking for nail and beauty services, and I wanted to talk through what a campaign could look like for your business, particularly if there are any slower windows during the week or, you know, capacity that you'd like to fill. We've had a lot of success with nail salons in the city and I'd love to set up some time to chat, walk you through what a campaign might look like, what the economics are, all of that. So if you could give me a call back, my number is — let me give you that — it's 312-555-0287, again 312-555-0287, my name again is Connor Hayes with DealDrop, and I look forward to connecting. Thanks so much, have a great day.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: [click]

scenario.notes: Owner ran DealDrop in 2021, decent experience but let it lapse because the volume overwhelmed her instructor schedule. She's open to it again if the rep can address capacity-pacing. The rep is genuinely good and this is closer to a textbook strong call — useful as a positive anchor in the dataset.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hey, am I speaking with Eleanor?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yes, this is Eleanor.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi Eleanor, this is Maya with DealDrop. I had time blocked to reach out today and I see you're between classes — is now still okay or should I find a different window?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Now's actually fine, I have about twenty minutes before my eleven o'clock.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Perfect. So I'll be upfront — I pulled your account before calling, I see Still Point ran with us in 2021, the campaign performed pretty well actually on raw numbers, and then it just sort of stopped. I wanted to ask you what happened from your side.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah. Honestly, the numbers were the problem. We sold — I want to say six hundred passes? Something like that. And our class capacity is twenty-four mats. We were turning away regular students because DealDrop people were taking the spots. And I had two instructors quit because they were burned out from teaching packed classes of beginners.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That's a really specific operational consequence and I appreciate you being direct about it. Can I ask — when you sold the six hundred passes, was there a redemption deadline, or were they good indefinitely?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: They were good for, I think, four months? Which sounded reasonable until everyone redeemed in the first six weeks.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Right. So one of the things that's changed — and I want to be careful not to oversell this, but it's a real change — we now let merchants set both a redemption window and a daily redemption cap. So you could say, you know, 'this voucher is good for six months, and I'll only honor twenty redemptions per week,' or per day, whatever pacing makes sense for your studio. That would have prevented the front-loaded crush you experienced.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Okay, that's actually meaningful. The pacing was the whole problem. Can the cap be set by class type or just overall?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Overall, today. Class-type level pacing isn't something we've built. So if you have a hot vinyasa class that's already always full and a midday slow flow that's underbooked, the deal would still let people redeem into the hot vinyasa unless you blocked it at your scheduling system level.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Hm. Well, my scheduling system has class-level enrollment caps anyway, so I could just keep my regular caps and let DealDrop people fill the spots that weren't filling.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah, that's exactly the move. You'd basically be using DealDrop to fill your soft spots and your existing system would protect the hard ones.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Okay. What about the burnout problem? My instructors aren't going to be happy if they hear we're doing this again.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That's not really my domain to solve, honestly. What I can tell you is that with pacing controls in place, the instructor-side experience is closer to a slow trickle of new students rather than the flash flood you got last time. Whether that's enough to address the team's concern is your judgment call.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Fair enough. I appreciate you not pretending you have an answer for that.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah. Can I ask about your current state — what does utilization look like? You said the eleven o'clock is upcoming, what's that class typically at?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Eleven AM weekday is usually like sixty, seventy percent. The five-thirty and six-thirty PM classes are at a hundred percent and have a waitlist. Saturday morning is full. Sunday is sleepy.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: So the underutilization is concentrated in midday weekday and Sunday.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yes, exactly.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Okay, that's actually a nice pattern for this — the people buying DealDrop are disproportionately people with flexible schedules or weekend warriors looking for a deal, so they tend to fill exactly those windows.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: That tracks with what I saw last time too, honestly.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: What's your current new-student acquisition channel mix?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Mostly Instagram and word of mouth. I do some local partnerships. I haven't done paid ads in like a year and a half.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Got it. So DealDrop would slot in as the paid acquisition channel, basically.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah, that's how I'd think about it.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Okay. I think there's a real conversation here. What I'd want to do is put together a campaign proposal with pacing built in, structured for the midday and Sunday windows specifically, and have you push back on the numbers. I'd want to do that as a thirty-minute call where I share my screen and we go through it together. Could we do that next week?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah, next week works. I prefer mornings. Tuesday or Thursday before noon?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Thursday at ten?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Thursday at ten.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I'll send the invite to the email on your website. Anything else I should pull or prep before then?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: If you can pull the actual campaign data from 2021, that would be useful. I'd like to see it next to whatever you're proposing.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I'll have it ready. Thanks Eleanor, looking forward to Thursday.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Thanks Maya.

scenario.notes: Owner is hostile from word one. Rep tries to push past it instead of accepting the no. Rep is end-of-quarter quota-pressured and reaches. This is genuinely rough — the rough-end of the 70/30 messy split. Rep does not handle this well.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi, am I speaking with the owner of Hickory Pit?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Who's calling.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: This is Marcus from DealDrop, I —

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: No.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I'm sorry?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: No. We're not interested. Take us off your list.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I understand you might have had a — can I just ask if you've worked with us before, because —

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yes. We worked with you. It was a disaster. I lost money on every plate for three months. Take us off the list.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I hear that, and I want to acknowledge that, and the platform has changed a lot since —

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I don't care. I am running a lunch service right now. I have people waiting for ribs. I told you no. What part of that —

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Sir, I'm just trying to —

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Goodbye.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: [click]

scenario.notes: GM is interested but doesn't have final say — owner is on vacation. Rep doesn't catch the authority limit until late, then tries to close anyway. Adversarial-adjacent: tests whether rubric distinguishes 'moved the call forward to the right person' from 'secured a meeting.'

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi, am I speaking with — let me see — is this Polish?

transcript.speaker: GM
transcript.text: Polish — uh, you mean Polish Blowdry Bar? This is Sam, I'm the GM.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hey Sam, this is Aaron from DealDrop. Got a few minutes?

transcript.speaker: GM
transcript.text: Yeah, sure. We're slow right now. What's up?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Awesome. So I've been looking at Polish — I love the branding by the way, the website's really clean — and I wanted to talk to you about running a DealDrop campaign. Have you guys ever worked with us?

transcript.speaker: GM
transcript.text: No, never. I think the owner's looked at it before but never pulled the trigger.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Got it, got it. So just to give you the quick rundown, DealDrop is the largest local commerce platform, we have a really big audience of LA consumers actively shopping for beauty services, and we work with merchants on running offers — discounted services basically — to drive new customer acquisition. For a place like Polish, what we typically see work really well is like a discounted blowout, like maybe a thirty-five-dollar blowout when your normal price is fifty, fifty-five, and that brings in net new customers who can then convert to your membership or rebook at full price.

transcript.speaker: GM
transcript.text: Yeah, that's interesting. Our normal blowout is forty-five.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Forty-five, okay. So we'd probably want to look at something in the high twenties, like twenty-eight, thirty bucks, with a really clean upsell path. We have campaign tools that let you set redemption windows, you can do rebook offers at the point of service, all that good stuff. We also have a feature now where you can cap daily redemptions which is really popular with salons because it prevents the flash flood thing where everyone redeems at once.

transcript.speaker: GM
transcript.text: That makes sense. We have time slots in our booking system anyway so I don't think the flood thing would happen but it's good to know.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Totally, totally. And the other thing I'd mention — we have really good reporting on the back end, so you can see exactly how many redemptions converted to repeat visits, what your customer lifetime value looks like coming through DealDrop, all that.

transcript.speaker: GM
transcript.text: Cool. I mean, this all sounds — yeah, I think we'd be open to looking at it. What do you need from us?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Awesome. So what I'd love to do is set up a thirty-minute call to walk through a campaign proposal — we'd put together specific offer structures, pricing, the whole thing, and you could give us feedback. Could we do that next week?

transcript.speaker: GM
transcript.text: I mean — I can do that, but you should know, I don't actually sign off on stuff like this. The owner Anna would need to be on that call. She's on vacation right now, she's back next Wednesday.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Oh, got it. Okay. Could we do it Thursday or Friday next week then?

transcript.speaker: GM
transcript.text: Probably, yeah. I'd want to check with her first, just to flag it. Can you send me an email with like a one-pager or something I can share with her?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah, I can do better than that — let me go ahead and put a hold on her calendar for Thursday at — let's say two PM — and I'll send the proposal in advance so she can review it.

transcript.speaker: GM
transcript.text: I — I don't have access to her calendar. I'd need to ask her.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Okay, no worries. Let me send you the meeting invite and the proposal, and you can forward both to her.

transcript.speaker: GM
transcript.text: Yeah, that works. I'll loop her in when she's back.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Awesome. What's your email?

transcript.speaker: GM
transcript.text: It's sam at polishblow dot co.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Polishblow dot co, got it. And Anna's?

transcript.speaker: GM
transcript.text: I don't want to give out her email without asking. Just send it to me, I'll forward.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Sounds good. I'll get that out today and we'll get something on the books for next week.

transcript.speaker: GM
transcript.text: Cool. Thanks Aaron.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Thanks Sam, talk soon.

scenario.notes: Long call, full discovery, both parties conclude it isn't a fit. Different from call_002 (which was a fast qualified DQ) — this one works the angles for 15+ minutes before the merchant and rep mutually recognize the math doesn't work. Tests whether rubric credits depth of inquiry when the answer is still no.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi, is this Devin?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Speaking.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi Devin, this is Hannah from DealDrop. I see you ran with us back in 2022 — I'm reaching out because I had your account on a follow-up list and wanted to see if it'd make sense to revisit. Got a few minutes?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I have a few minutes, yeah. We did run with you guys, we let it lapse. I'd be willing to talk through it but I'll tell you upfront I'm pretty skeptical that it's the right channel for us anymore.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah, totally fair. Do you mind if I ask what's changed since 2022?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah. So when we ran with you, we were a year and a half in, we had two rooms, we were trying to fill weekday afternoon slots. The DealDrop campaign worked, sort of — we filled the slots. But the customers we got were really price-sensitive, they didn't tip the GMs, they left mediocre reviews when they didn't beat the room, and we ended up pulling the plug after about four months. Since then we've added two more rooms, we have a really strong corporate channel now, and Friday/Saturday/Sunday we're booked solid. Weekday afternoon is still soft but it's more soft than it used to be.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Okay, that's a really clear picture. Can I ask — what's your average ticket per booking right now?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: We charge thirty-five per person, and the rooms are six to eight people. So a booking is anywhere from two-ten to two-eighty. Corporate bookings we charge more, more like forty to forty-five per head plus a buyout fee.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Got it. And on a typical Tuesday or Wednesday afternoon, what's your utilization look like?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Maybe forty percent of the slots fill organically. Sixty percent of them just sit empty.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: So you've got real unsold capacity in the windows where DealDrop people would tend to redeem. The question I'd be asking myself in your shoes is whether the unit economics work — whether bringing a deal-priced booking in is better than the empty room, and how the customer-quality issue you described before plays into that.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah, that's exactly the question. And the answer last time was no. The empty rooms are actually fine — there's no marginal cost to me, the GMs are on shift anyway because we have to staff for walk-ins, the lights are on. So the alternative isn't 'no revenue' it's 'no revenue but also no headache.' The DealDrop customers brought headache.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Right. So the cost side of an empty room is essentially zero, and the cost side of a DealDrop redemption includes the deal margin loss plus the experience tax of dealing with a lower-quality customer.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Exactly.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Can I push on one thing? Has your customer mix changed enough that you'd actually be reaching different people now? Like — were the DealDrop buyers in 2022 specifically a 'I want to spend twenty bucks on a Saturday activity' crowd, or was it more general?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Mostly the former. Younger, often first-time escape room people, often groups of friends rather than couples or work groups. They didn't really come back. The corporate channel and the date-night/special-occasion crowd, those are repeat business. The DealDrop crowd was one-and-done.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That's — yeah, that's a structural problem with the channel for your business. The customers who buy through DealDrop are disproportionately deal hunters by selection. You can soften it with targeting and offer design but you can't fully fix it.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Right. That's where I landed last time too.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Let me ask one more — have you considered or tried using us specifically for a new room launch or a new product? Like if you opened a fifth room or launched, I don't know, a special seasonal experience, sometimes the channel works better as a 'drive trial of this specific new thing' play rather than as ongoing capacity fill.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I have thought about that. We're actually launching a holiday room in November. It's a Christmas-themed escape room that we'll only run for six weeks.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Okay, that's — that's actually closer to a fit. Limited-time, specific launch, you'd be using DealDrop as awareness and trial-driver for something with built-in scarcity. The customer-quality concern matters less because the room itself is going away.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Hmm. I — I'd consider that. But I'd want to think about it carefully. Last time burned us enough that I'm not going to jump in.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Totally fair. And I want to be honest — for your core business, ongoing weekly campaigns, I don't think DealDrop is the right fit and I'm not going to pretend otherwise. The holiday room is a different conversation. Would it be worth a thirty-minute follow-up specifically about that? I could put together what a six-week launch campaign would look like and you could tell me if it makes sense.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah, you know what, sure. The holiday thing is a different question and I'd hear it out. But just for that.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Just for that. Got it. What's your timing — when do you typically start marketing the holiday room?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: We start teasing it mid-October. So really we'd want to make a decision by the end of September.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Let me come back to you the week of September fifteenth with a proposal specifically for the holiday room. Does that work?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: That works.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I'll email a confirmation today. Thanks for being honest about the 2022 experience, by the way — that helps me not waste your time on the wrong pitch.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah, no problem. Thanks Hannah.

scenario.notes: Adversarial-adjacent: rep is genuinely friendly and likable, the merchant likes him. Rep says yes to a meeting at the end. But there is essentially zero discovery, and the rep makes confident claims about how the deal will work for handyman services that aren't grounded in anything. Tests whether the rubric distinguishes 'warm rapport + booked meeting' from actual selling competence.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hello, am I speaking with Bill?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: This is Bill, who's this?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hey Bill, this is Tommy calling from DealDrop, how you doing today?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Doing alright, doing alright. What can I do for you?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Well Bill, I wanted to talk to you about getting more customers in the door for Bill's Handyman Service. You ever heard of DealDrop?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I've heard of it. My daughter uses it, gets restaurant deals and stuff. I never thought about it for the business though.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah, see, that's exactly why I'm calling. Most handymen never think about it but it actually works really well for guys like you. We have a huge audience of homeowners in Chicago looking for home services, and what we do is put your services in front of them at a discount — get them to call you the first time — and then you've got them as a customer for life because, you know, once someone has a handyman they trust, they keep calling that handyman.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah, that part's true.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Right? So what we'd typically do for a handyman is something like, you know, two hours of work for a discounted rate, or like a — like a TV mounting deal or a faucet install, something like that, that gets them in the door, and then once you're there you can quote them on whatever else they need.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Mm-hmm, mm-hmm. Hold on a sec — Yeah, I'll be right out — sorry, my guy needs me. What were you saying?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: No problem, no problem. So we'd structure something to get you in the door with new customers, and then the upsell happens naturally on-site.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: That part makes sense. I do that anyway when I'm at a job. Half the time the lady has me fix three things she didn't tell me about when she called.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Exactly! Exactly. So you already get it. The DealDrop just brings them in.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: How much does it cost? On my end I mean.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: There's no upfront cost. We take a percentage of each redemption. So the way it works is the customer buys the deal through us, we keep a portion, and you get the rest.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: What's the portion?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: It varies depending on the campaign and the category but typically it's around fifty percent.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Fifty percent of the deal price?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah, of the discounted price.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: So if I'm doing a — let's say I'm doing a hundred-dollar TV mount and the deal is fifty bucks off, that's a fifty-dollar deal, you take twenty-five and I get twenty-five?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Right, but the way to think about it is the twenty-five is your customer acquisition cost, and once they're a customer they're calling you for everything else.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I mean — I'd be losing seventy-five bucks on the first job is what you're saying.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: You're investing seventy-five bucks to acquire a customer who's going to be worth way more than that over time.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah, I see what you're saying. Look, I'm not saying no, but I'd want to see the numbers written down. I'm not great with the phone for this kind of thing. Can you come by? Or — like a meeting?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah, absolutely, I can set up a call with one of our campaign specialists who'll walk through everything in detail. Would early next week work?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah, early next week's fine.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: How about Tuesday at ten?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Tuesday at ten. I'll be in between jobs probably, but yeah, give me a call.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Awesome, I'll get that scheduled. What's the best email for the calendar invite?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I don't really do calendar invites. Just call this number.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: You got it Bill. Tuesday at ten, this number.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Sounds good. Take care now.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: You too Bill.

scenario.notes: Spouse picks up — partner of the actual owner-chef. Has decision influence but won't commit without the chef. Already-busy ramen shop doesn't need volume. Rep does decent discovery, surfaces that this isn't an obvious fit, but offers a specific framing that might still work (slow Tuesdays, lunch deal not dinner). Mutual 'let me think about it / discuss with my husband' close.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi, am I speaking with the owner of Tonkotsu House?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: This is — I am Kenji's wife, Aiko. He is in the kitchen. Can I help you?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi Aiko, this is Diego with DealDrop. I was hoping to talk to whoever handles marketing for the restaurant. Is that you, or would that be Kenji?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: We both. He is the chef, I do the front and the — paperwork, marketing. What is this about please?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Sure. So I'm reaching out because I work with restaurants in the city on running promotional campaigns — basically getting new customers in the door through DealDrop's audience. I wanted to see if it'd be worth a few minutes to talk about whether something like that could make sense for you. Is now an okay time? I know you're probably about to open.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: We open at five thirty for dinner. It is — three thirty now, so I have some time. But please — short, because the soup is cooking and I need to check.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Totally. So before I pitch anything, can I ask a couple of questions about the restaurant?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Sure.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: How busy are you typically on a weeknight? Like a Tuesday or Wednesday at seven PM, what does that look like?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Tuesday, Wednesday — usually full. We have forty-two seats. We turn maybe twice for dinner. So eighty, ninety covers on a normal Tuesday.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Wow, okay. So you're not really running into capacity issues.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: No. Friday, Saturday we have a wait outside. We are very busy.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Got it. Have you ever felt like you needed more customers, or is it more about something else?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Honestly, no. The problem is not customers. The problem is — we are tired. My husband, the kitchen is small, we have two cooks. We do not want more covers, we want — we want the same covers but more — more profit per cover maybe? Or we want to slow Mondays.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Mondays are slow?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Mondays we are closed actually. Tuesday lunch is slow. Tuesday dinner is okay. Tuesday lunch, maybe twenty covers. We are open eleven thirty to two thirty for lunch.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Okay, so Tuesday lunch specifically is the soft spot.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yes.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That's actually useful. Most of what DealDrop does well is filling specific underutilized windows. Driving Friday night customers when you already have a wait is the wrong move — that just trades full-price customers for discounted ones. But filling a Tuesday lunch when you'd otherwise be at a third of capacity, that math is different. The customers buying a Tuesday lunch deal aren't replacing someone who would have paid full price — they're net new revenue in a slot that was empty.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: That makes sense. But I worry — DealDrop people, I have heard, they expect very cheap. They are not loyal. They come once and go.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That's a real pattern and I won't lie to you about it. The way it can work is if the offer is structured for repeat — like, instead of just a discounted bowl, it's a 'buy a bowl get a second bowl half off, valid Tuesday lunch only,' or something that brings them back twice. The first visit is the trial, the second visit is where you actually find out if they're going to become a regular.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Hmm. I would have to talk to my husband. He is — he does not like discounts. He thinks the food should be the price it is.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That's a totally legitimate position and I respect it. Can I ask — would you be open to me putting together a one-page proposal specifically for Tuesday lunch, that you could review with him? That way it's not me trying to convince him on a phone call, it's something concrete he can look at and react to.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yes, that I can do. Send me something to look at, I will show him, and then I call you back if we want to talk more.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That works. What's the best email?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Aiko at tonkotsuhouse-nyc dot com.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Got it. I'll send something over by end of week. And just so I'm clear on what I'm putting together — I'm not trying to sell you anything broader than Tuesday lunch. I think Friday dinner DealDrop would be a mistake for you.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: You are honest, that is good. Okay, send the Tuesday lunch only.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Tuesday lunch only. Thanks Aiko.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Thank you. I have to check the soup. Bye bye.

scenario.notes: Same merchant as call_005 (Splinter Axe Co). They got past the gatekeeper this time — owner Trevor picks up. He's already a DealDrop customer, has an existing rep, and is annoyed that this is the second cold call this month from someone at DealDrop who didn't know that. CRM data quality fail on DealDrop's end. Rep tries to recover by pitching a campaign upgrade or expansion — partially works, partially doesn't.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi, am I trying to reach the owner of Splinter Axe Co?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: This is Trevor, I'm the owner. Who's calling?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi Trevor, this is Priya from DealDrop. Wanted to talk to you about — uh, about a campaign for the business, do you have a couple minutes?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Are you serious?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I'm sorry?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: We already run with DealDrop. We have for like a year. I have a rep, her name is Lauren. This is the second time someone from DealDrop has called me cold this month and I'm — honestly, I'm pretty annoyed about it.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I — okay, I apologize for that. Let me — let me check on my end —

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I don't really care about the explanation. The data on your end is bad. I have a rep. Why am I getting cold calls.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That's a totally fair frustration and I'm sorry. Can I — let me ask a different question. Since I have you on the phone, do you mind if I ask how things are going with the current campaign?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I mean, fine. It's — it's fine.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Just fine?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah, just fine. We get bookings through it, redemption rate's okay, but honestly Lauren has been telling me for like four months that she wants to talk about expanding into corporate group bookings as a separate campaign and we keep not getting it on the calendar.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Okay. So there's an expansion conversation that's been stalled.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Look — I don't want to step on Lauren's account, that's not my place, and I'd be annoyed if someone did that to me. But would it be useful if I flagged this internally so you actually get the corporate conversation booked? Like, I can put a note on your account that there's an expansion opportunity that's been pending and ping the team that owns your relationship.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I mean — sure. Yeah. That'd be useful actually. Lauren is good, she's just busy.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Got it. Can I ask just a couple of quick things about the corporate angle, just so the note is useful?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: A couple. I'm at the front right now, my host just walked into the back.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Quick. Have you done corporate group bookings before?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah, organic ones. Bachelor parties, team-building stuff, holiday parties especially in Q4. We're priced higher for those because we customize, we cater food in, we do a private session. It's a real margin product for us.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: And you're thinking of a DealDrop campaign that drives more of those specifically?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah. Lauren's idea was a corporate-targeted offer, basically a 'team-building event for ten or more, X dollars off when booked thirty days out' type thing.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That makes sense. Different audience, different campaign mechanics, different reporting. You'd want it as a separate line item from your current consumer campaign.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Right. That's what she said.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Okay. I'll flag this to Lauren today, and I'll specifically note that the corporate expansion conversation has been waiting on a calendar slot and it sounds like Q4 is the relevant window.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: That'd actually be helpful. Sorry I was short with you.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: No, no. The data quality issue is real and your reaction made sense. I'll get this flagged. Anything else you want me to pass along?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: No, just — get Lauren on the phone with me, that's the ask.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I'll do that. Thanks Trevor.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Thanks Priya.

scenario.notes: Follow-up to call_008. This is the Thursday 10 AM proposal call between Maya and Eleanor. Eleanor has slept on it, talked to her instructors (it didn't go well), and shows up to the call more skeptical than she ended on. Maya has to navigate fresh objections, walk through real numbers, and close on a smaller pilot rather than the full campaign. Long, substantive, multiple thread shifts.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hey Eleanor, this is Maya. Can you see my screen okay?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yep, I see it. The proposal doc.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Great. So before I walk through anything — how have you been thinking about this since last week? Any new questions or concerns come up?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah. Honestly, I — I have to be straight with you. I talked to my two senior instructors over the weekend and they're both pretty against this. One of them was here when we did DealDrop in 2021 and she still remembers the burnout. The other one wasn't here then but heard the stories.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Okay. That's important. Did they raise specific concerns or was it more general resistance?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Both. The general thing is — they're worried I'm signaling that we're a discount studio, which is a brand thing. The specific thing is the pacing. Even with caps, they're worried that the people we attract will be — like, they remember teaching classes that were full of beginners who didn't really know what they were doing, and the regulars felt crowded out.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Both of those are real and I want to take them one at a time. Is that okay?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah, please.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: On the brand thing — what's your current pricing structure?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Drop-in is twenty-eight, ten-class pack is two-forty, monthly unlimited is two-twenty.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: And what's your typical DealDrop-style offer in your category in NYC? You see other yoga studios advertising what?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I see a lot of 'ten classes for forty-nine dollars' type things. Which is — yeah, it's deep discounting and it does signal something.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Right. So one of the things I want to propose, and this is different from how we ran in 2021, is we don't do a deep-discount intro pack. We do a 'first-month unlimited at a discount' structure — say, one-fifty for a first-month unlimited, instead of two-twenty. That's a thirty-two percent discount, not a sixty or seventy percent discount, and the framing is 'trial month' rather than 'ten cheap classes.'

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: That's — that's a more defensible price point.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: It also self-selects for a different customer. Someone who's willing to pay one-fifty for a month of unlimited yoga is making a more serious commitment than someone who buys a ten-pack for forty-nine. They're more likely to actually become a regular.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: That tracks. Okay. So that addresses the brand piece, kind of. What about the instructor concern about beginners?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah. So — can I be honest about a tradeoff here?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Please.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: A first-month-unlimited offer is going to attract people who are deeper into yoga than a ten-pack-for-forty-nine offer would. You'll get fewer absolute beginners. But you'll still get some. Some people use the first month to try yoga seriously for the first time. There's no version of this where the answer is zero beginners.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Right.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: The question I'd want to ask back is — does your studio have classes that are appropriate for beginners? Like, are there levels?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah, we have basics classes specifically for newer students. They're at off-peak times — Tuesday and Thursday at noon, Saturday at nine AM.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Okay. So one option here is we structure the deal so it specifically routes new redeemers toward the basics classes for their first two weeks. We can put language in the deal — 'first two weeks, basics classes only; weeks three and four, all classes.' That way the burnout-on-instructors problem in your peak classes is mitigated, your basics classes — which I'm guessing are underutilized — get filled, and people graduate into the full schedule once they have some baseline competence.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: That's actually — that's a smart structure. The basics classes are like thirty percent full right now.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Right. So you'd be using the campaign to fill basics class capacity, which is a soft spot, while protecting your full-tier schedule which is your money-maker and where the instructor strain came from last time.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Okay. I want to push back on one thing though. The 2021 campaign — we had six hundred passes redeem in six weeks. If this campaign hits at anywhere near that volume, even with the basics-class routing, my Tuesday and Thursday noon and my Saturday morning are going to be packed. My senior instructors teach those classes too.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Fair. Two things on that. One is the cap — we'd set a daily redemption cap, and I'd want to set it conservatively, like ten new redemptions per day for the first month, then evaluate. Two is the volume from a one-fifty offer is just structurally lower than a forty-nine-dollar offer. The 2021 campaign sold six hundred passes because it was cheap. This one will sell fewer just because the price point is higher.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: How many fewer, in your experience?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I'd want to be careful not to give you a number I can't back up. What I can tell you is across studios doing first-month-unlimited at this price range, we typically see total volume that's somewhere between a quarter and a half of what a deep-discount intro pack would do. So instead of six hundred, you might see one-fifty to three hundred over the campaign. With a cap of ten per day, even the high end would land at a manageable pace.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Okay. One sec — yeah, go ahead, I'll be out in fifteen — okay, I'm back. Sorry.
transcript.stage_direction: pause, sound of door, voice in background — 'Eleanor, do you want me to start the four PM playlist?'

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: No worries.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I want to ask the hard question. What if I do this and it goes badly? What's my exposure?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Real question. Your exposure is — you'll have committed to honoring redemptions for the validity period, which I'd recommend setting at four months. So if you launch the campaign and at week three you're hating it, you can pull the deal from sale, but the passes that were already bought have to be honored. That's the exposure.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: So if I sell one-fifty in the first three weeks and pull it, I have those one-fifty redemptions to honor over the next three months.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Exactly.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: That's actually — that's not as bad as I was imagining.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah. The exposure is bounded by the cap and the validity window. It's not infinite.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Okay. Look — I'm not going to commit on this call. I want to take this back to my instructors with the basics-class routing structure, the lower volume estimate, and the cap. If they're still no, I'm going to listen to them. But I think this is more answerable than where we started.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That's exactly the right way to handle it. Can I ask — what would help your conversation with them?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Honestly, if you can put together a one-page summary of what we just discussed — the offer structure, the cap, the basics-class routing, the volume estimate, the exposure — I'd want to share that, not the full proposal.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I can have that to you tomorrow morning.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: That's perfect.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: One more thing before we wrap. What's the realistic timeline if you do decide to move forward? Like, what's your decision process?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I'd want to make a call within two weeks. If we're going to launch I'd want it live before Labor Day for a fall push.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Okay. So let's set a follow-up for two weeks from today, on the assumption that you've talked to your team. If you're a no, you can email me before then and we cancel the call. If you're a yes, we use that call to finalize.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: That works.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I'll send the one-pager and the calendar invite today. Thanks for being honest about the instructor pushback — that's exactly the conversation we needed to have.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yeah. Thanks Maya.

scenario.notes: Follow-up to call_006. Thursday 3 PM proposal call between Jordan and Mariana. Mariana has read the proposal, has done her own math, and is pushing back hard on specific numbers. Her husband Carlos is in the room and chimes in. Jordan's prior over-promise from the first call ('30% rebooking is the average for cleaning') becomes the central tension — Mariana looked it up and is calling him on it. How he handles the caught-out moment is the test.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi Mariana, it's Jordan. Did you get a chance to look at the proposal I sent?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yes, I read it. Carlos read it too — my husband. He is here with me.

transcript.speaker: Spouse
transcript.text: Hello.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Hi Carlos, nice to e-meet you.

transcript.speaker: Spouse
transcript.text: Yes.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: So — we have questions. And some concerns.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That's exactly what this call is for. Where do you want to start?

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: I want to start with the rebooking number. You told me on the first call that thirty percent is the average for cleaning when the merchant uses post-service follow-up. I looked this up. I called two friends who are also in cleaning, one in Phoenix and one in San Diego, who have run DealDrop. They both said their rebook rate was much lower. Like, ten percent, fifteen percent. And I found a forum online where cleaning business owners were saying the same thing. So where does the thirty percent come from?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Okay. I — I appreciate you doing that homework, and I want to be straight with you. The thirty percent number I gave you was the upper end of what I've seen, not the average. I should have been more careful with how I framed that. I'm sorry.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Mm.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: The honest range, based on what I actually have visibility into, is closer to what your friends said for most merchants. Ten to twenty percent rebooking is more typical. Thirty percent is achievable but it's the top end and it requires aggressive follow-up — like, point-of-service rebook offers, email sequences, a real customer-experience effort. It's not the average and I shouldn't have said it was.

transcript.speaker: Spouse
transcript.text: So why did you say it was the average.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That's a fair question. Honestly — I was leaning forward into the call, I'd seen some campaigns that hit those numbers, and I generalized from the strong ones. That was wrong of me. I'd rather lose your business by being honest now than win it by misleading you.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Okay. I appreciate that. I am — I am not happy that the first number you gave me was wrong, but I appreciate that you said it instead of trying to defend it.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah. So let me — let me redo the math with the honest number, and you tell me if it still works for you.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yes. Please.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Last call, you told me your three-eighty deep clean has about one-sixty in cost — labor, supplies, gas, insurance. That leaves two-twenty in margin before overhead. At a forty percent off DealDrop deal, you'd be charging two-twenty-eight. After DealDrop's cut at fifty percent of that, you receive about one-fourteen. Your cost is one-sixty. So you'd be losing forty-six dollars on the first job.

transcript.speaker: Spouse
transcript.text: Yes. That is what I calculated last night.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Right. So the entire economic argument depends on rebooking. At a fifteen percent rebook rate — using the more honest number — for every hundred DealDrop customers, you'd get fifteen who book a second time at full price. The math is: a hundred customers, you lose forty-six on each first job, that's negative forty-six hundred dollars. Fifteen of them rebook at three-eighty with one-sixty cost, that's plus thirty-three hundred. Net is roughly negative thirteen hundred over a hundred customers.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: That is what we got too.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah. Even if a third of those fifteen book a third time, you'd be looking at maybe break-even or slight positive. It's a thin economic case at fifteen percent.

transcript.speaker: Spouse
transcript.text: So the deal is bad for us.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: At forty percent off and a fifteen percent rebook rate, with no other levers pulled, yes. The deal is structurally bad.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Then what — what is the proposal here? You sent me a proposal for a forty percent off campaign.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Let me think about this with you out loud. The proposal I sent assumed a higher rebook rate than is realistic for most merchants. With the honest rate, the deal as structured doesn't work. So either the deal needs to be structured differently or the rebook rate needs to be higher.

transcript.speaker: Spouse
transcript.text: How would the rebook rate be higher.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That's the question. The honest answer is — there are things you can do at the point of service that move the rebook rate. A handwritten 'ten percent off your next clean within thirty days' card handed to the customer when the team finishes. A follow-up email two days after the clean asking how it went and offering rebook. A specific person on your team whose job is to call within a week. Things like that have moved rebook rates from twelve to twenty in studies I've seen. But that's effort on your side, and there's no guarantee.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: So you are saying I would have to do extra work to make this deal break even.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Honestly? Yes. With a fifteen percent baseline rebook and a forty percent discount, you would need to invest in customer-experience work to push the rebook rate to twenty or higher to make the campaign positive. That's the real picture.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Carlos says — and I agree — we cannot do that extra work right now. We are — we are running the business and we have one part-time admin. We do not have time to build a follow-up system.
transcript.stage_direction: says something to Mariana in Spanish, she responds, brief exchange

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: That's a totally legitimate constraint, and I want to honor it. So given that, I don't think the campaign as proposed is the right move for you right now.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: You are telling me not to do the deal.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: I'm telling you that the deal as I structured it, given your honest rebook rate and your operational capacity, is unlikely to make money. I would not feel good about you running it.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Okay. Jordan — I have to be honest with you. I came into this call ready to argue with you about your numbers, and I was prepared to walk away. I did not expect you to agree with me that the deal does not work.
transcript.stage_direction: longer pause, then to Carlos in Spanish, he responds

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yeah.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: But — I do not want to close the door completely. If we — if we did build that follow-up system, in like six months, would you talk to me again?

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Yes, absolutely. And honestly, the right play if you do that work would be a smaller campaign first — like a thirty-day pilot, capped at thirty redemptions, just to get real data on your specific rebook rate before we commit to something bigger. I'd rather do that than re-launch the same proposal.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: That makes sense.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Tell you what — let me make a note to follow up with you in five months. By then you'll know if you've been able to build the follow-up system or not. If you have, we talk. If you haven't, no hard feelings.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Yes. Five months. That works for me.

transcript.speaker: Spouse
transcript.text: Thank you Jordan. We appreciate the honesty.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Thank you both. Mariana, I owe you the apology again — I should have given you the honest number on the first call. I'll be more careful with that going forward.

transcript.speaker: Merchant
transcript.text: Okay. Thank you. Five months.

transcript.speaker: Rep
transcript.text: Five months. Take care.
